name: jest
context: Write and run JavaScript/TypeScript tests with Jest — mocking, snapshots, coverage, watch mode, and CI integration
info: Jest is the standard JavaScript and TypeScript test runner, created by Meta. It provides a zero-config experience for most projects with built-in mocking (jest.fn, jest.mock, jest.spyOn), snapshot testing, code coverage via Istanbul/V8, interactive --watch mode, and parallel test execution across worker processes. Configuration lives in jest.config.js/ts or the "jest" key in package.json. Jest discovers test files matching *.test.js/ts or *.spec.js/ts and provides globals (describe, it/test, expect, beforeEach, afterEach, beforeAll, afterAll) without imports. TypeScript support comes via ts-jest or @swc/jest transforms. The current stable release is Jest 29.x. For ESM-first Vite projects, consider [[testing/vitest]] as a faster alternative with a Jest-compatible API. Jest integrates with [[testing/mocking]] patterns for isolating units and [[testing/ci]] for running suites in automated pipelines. See [[typescript/compiler]] for TypeScript compilation settings that affect test transforms.
triggers: run tests in CI with jest

sections.title: Quick start
sections.body: Install Jest with `npm install --save-dev jest` (add `@types/jest` for TypeScript). Create a `*.test.js` or `*.test.ts` file. Write a test using `describe`, `it`/`test`, and `expect`. Run with `npx jest`. Add `"test": "jest"` to package.json scripts. For TypeScript, install `ts-jest` and set `preset: 'ts-jest'` in jest.config.js, or use `@swc/jest` for faster transforms. First test: `test('adds numbers', () => { expect(1 + 2).toBe(3); });`

sections.title: Configuration
sections.body: Jest config lives in `jest.config.js` (or `.ts`, `.mjs`, `.cjs`) or the `"jest"` key in `package.json`. Key options: `testMatch` (glob patterns for test files), `testEnvironment` ('node' or 'jsdom'), `transform` (file transforms like ts-jest or @swc/jest), `moduleNameMapper` (path aliases and asset mocks), `setupFilesAfterSetup` (run before each suite), `clearMocks`/`restoreMocks` (auto-cleanup between tests), `collectCoverageFrom` (files to include in coverage), `coverageThreshold` (minimum coverage gates). For monorepos, use `projects` array to run multiple configs. Use `--config` flag to specify a non-default config file.

sections.title: Matchers
sections.body: Jest's `expect()` API provides matchers for assertions. Primitives: `toBe()` (===), `toBeTruthy()`, `toBeFalsy()`, `toBeNull()`, `toBeUndefined()`, `toBeDefined()`. Numbers: `toBeGreaterThan()`, `toBeLessThan()`, `toBeCloseTo()`. Strings: `toMatch(/regex/)`, `toContain('substring')`. Objects: `toEqual()` (deep), `toStrictEqual()` (deep + undefined props), `toMatchObject()` (subset), `toHaveProperty('path')`. Arrays: `toContain()`, `toHaveLength()`, `toContainEqual()`. Errors: `toThrow()`, `toThrow('message')`, `toThrow(ErrorType)`. All matchers support `.not` for negation. Async: `await expect(promise).resolves.toBe(v)` and `await expect(promise).rejects.toThrow(e)`. Mock matchers: `toHaveBeenCalled()`, `toHaveBeenCalledWith()`, `toHaveBeenCalledTimes()`, `toHaveBeenLastCalledWith()`, `toHaveBeenNthCalledWith()`.

sections.title: Mocking
sections.body: Jest provides three mocking mechanisms. `jest.fn()` creates standalone mock functions with configurable return values (`mockReturnValue`, `mockResolvedValue`, `mockImplementation`) and call tracking (`toHaveBeenCalled`, `toHaveBeenCalledWith`). `jest.mock('module')` replaces an entire module's exports with auto-mocked versions or a manual factory function. Use `jest.requireActual()` inside a factory to keep some exports real. `jest.spyOn(object, 'method')` wraps an existing method with a spy, preserving the original implementation unless overridden. Always call `mockRestore()` or set `restoreMocks: true` in config to prevent leaks. For manual mocks, create `__mocks__/module.ts` adjacent to the real module. Timer mocking: `jest.useFakeTimers()`, `jest.advanceTimersByTime(ms)`, `jest.runAllTimers()`. Module mocking hoists to the top of the file — `jest.mock()` calls are moved above imports by Jest's transform.

sections.title: Snapshot testing
sections.body: Snapshot testing captures output and compares against stored snapshots to detect regressions. Use `expect(value).toMatchSnapshot()` to write to a `.snap` file in `__snapshots__/`, or `toMatchInlineSnapshot()` to store the snapshot inline in the test file. On first run, Jest creates the snapshot. On subsequent runs, it compares and fails if the output changed. Update snapshots with `npx jest -u` or `npx jest --updateSnapshot`. Use property matchers for dynamic values: `expect(obj).toMatchSnapshot({ id: expect.any(String), createdAt: expect.any(Date) })`. Always commit `.snap` files to version control. Review snapshot diffs in PRs carefully — large snapshots can hide regressions. Prefer inline snapshots for small values and `.snap` files for large structures like rendered component trees.

sections.title: Coverage
sections.body: Run `npx jest --coverage` to generate code coverage reports. Jest uses Istanbul by default (or V8 with `coverageProvider: 'v8'` in config). Configure `collectCoverageFrom` to specify which files to measure and `coveragePathIgnorePatterns` to exclude files. Set `coverageThreshold` in config to enforce minimums per metric (branches, functions, lines, statements) globally or per directory. Coverage reporters: `text` (terminal), `lcov` (HTML + LCOV for CI), `json`, `clover`. The `text` reporter shows a table with Stmts, Branch, Funcs, Lines, and Uncovered Line #s. For CI, use `lcov` and upload to Codecov or Coveralls. Enforce thresholds in CI to prevent coverage regression.

sections.title: TypeScript setup
sections.body: Two main approaches for TypeScript with Jest. **ts-jest**: Install `ts-jest @types/jest`, set `preset: 'ts-jest'` in jest.config. Respects tsconfig.json, supports type checking during tests (slow but catches type errors). Configure `tsconfig` option in ts-jest to use a test-specific tsconfig. **@swc/jest**: Install `@swc/jest @swc/core @types/jest`, set `transform: { '^.+\\.tsx?$': '@swc/jest' }`. Much faster than ts-jest but does not type-check (pair with `tsc --noEmit` in CI). Both approaches support path aliases via `moduleNameMapper` matching tsconfig `paths`. For projects using ES modules and TypeScript, SWC is the recommended path for speed.

sections.title: Watch mode
sections.body: Run `npx jest --watch` to enter interactive watch mode. Jest detects changed files via git and re-runs only related tests. Use `--watchAll` to re-run all tests on any change. Watch mode keybindings: `a` run all tests, `f` run only failed tests, `p` filter by filename regex, `t` filter by test name regex, `q` quit, `Enter` trigger a re-run. Watch mode requires a git or hg repository to detect changes. In CI environments (`CI=true`), watch mode is automatically disabled. Combine with `--verbose` for detailed output during development. Watch mode respects `--testPathPattern` and `--testNamePattern` for focused development.

scripts.name: Run Jest tests
scripts.description: Detect Jest installation, run tests with optional pattern filtering, coverage, watch mode, and verbose output. Handles CI mode automatically.

test_pattern.description: Test file path or name pattern to run (e.g. 'user-service' or 'src/utils')

passed.description: Number of test suites that passed

failed.description: Number of test suites that failed

tests_passed.description: Number of individual tests that passed

tests_failed.description: Number of individual tests that failed

duration_seconds.description: Total test run duration in seconds

depends.reason: TypeScript projects need tsconfig.json and a transform (ts-jest or @swc/jest)
The run-tests script requires bash to execute
